name: pub-sub
context: Publish and subscribe to Redis channels for real-time messaging
info: This skill covers Redis pub/sub: publishing messages to named channels, subscribing to channels for real-time delivery, pattern-based subscriptions for wildcard matching, and reliability considerations. Redis pub/sub is fire-and-forget — messages are delivered to all currently connected subscribers but are not persisted. For durable messaging with replay, use [[redis/streams]] instead. The ioredis client requires a dedicated connection for subscriptions (a subscriber connection cannot issue other commands). Pub/sub works across cluster nodes and is commonly used for cache invalidation, real-time notifications, and inter-service communication.

sections.title: Publish and subscribe
sections.body: Use `SUBSCRIBE channel` to listen and `PUBLISH channel message` to send. `PUBLISH` returns the number of subscribers who received the message. In ioredis, the subscriber connection fires `message` events with channel and message arguments. A subscriber connection is dedicated and cannot issue non-pub/sub commands. See [[redis#connection]] for connection setup.

sections.title: Pattern-based subscriptions
sections.body: Use `PSUBSCRIBE pattern` to match channels with glob patterns: `*` (any characters), `?` (one character), `[abc]` (character class). Pattern subscribers receive messages on the `pmessage` event with pattern, channel, and message. Useful for namespaced channels like `user:*:events` or `service.*`.

sections.title: Reliability and limitations
sections.body: Pub/sub is fire-and-forget: no persistence, no replay, no acknowledgment. Messages are lost if no subscriber is connected. For durable messaging, use [[redis/streams]] which provides append-only logs, consumer groups, and XACK acknowledgment. Pub/sub is ideal for real-time broadcast where occasional message loss is acceptable.

sections.title: Use cases
sections.body: Cache invalidation across instances (see [[redis/caching#invalidation]]). Real-time notifications via WebSocket bridges. Inter-service event bus in microservice architectures. Configuration propagation across worker fleets. Chat and presence systems with channel-per-room design.
